passageSets.title: Whose Grammar Is Correct?
passageSets.passage:
People speak confidently of 'correct' and 'incorrect' grammar, as though the distinction were as settled as arithmetic. A given construction is proper English or it is a mistake, a sign of carelessness or poor schooling, and the educated speaker's task is simply to avoid the errors. This picture is deeply held and, as a description of how language actually works, almost entirely wrong. What passes for correctness is far less a matter of logic than of power, and mistaking the one for the other has consequences worth tracing.

Begin with the linguist's basic finding. Every human community that speaks at all speaks a variety governed by rules, intricate rules that its speakers follow without being able to state them. The dialects that prestige dismisses as 'broken' or 'lazy' are, under inspection, as rule-bound as any other, often preserving distinctions the prestige dialect has lost. A construction condemned as a double negative in one variety is, in that variety, a consistent system for emphasizing negation, no more illogical than the way many admired languages handle the same task. Judged as systems, no dialect is broken; each does what its speakers need.

Why, then, does one variety come to be called correct? Not because it is clearer or more logical, for it is neither, but because it is the variety spoken by those who already hold power. The speech of the dominant region, class, or group is elevated into the standard, and its features are retroactively declared to be the rules of the language itself. The process is invisible to those it benefits, who experience their own dialect simply as 'proper English' and everyone else's as a falling-away from it. Correctness, on this analysis, is a social verdict wearing the costume of a linguistic one.

Yet it would be naive, and unkind, to leap from this to the cheerful conclusion that the distinction does not matter. It matters enormously, precisely because the verdict is social. A speaker whose dialect is stigmatized pays real costs, in schools, interviews, and courtrooms, whatever the linguist proves about the equality of systems. To tell such a speaker that correctness is a myth is true in theory and useless in practice, for the myth is enforced by people with the power to enforce it. Recognizing that a hierarchy is arbitrary does not, by itself, dissolve the hierarchy or spare those it penalizes.

The clear-eyed position holds two truths at once. As a matter of linguistic fact, no dialect is superior, and the language of correctness is a disguise for social rank. As a matter of social fact, that disguise has power, and speakers must reckon with it whether or not they endorse it. What follows is not resignation but a sharper target for reform: if the standard's authority rests on power rather than logic, then the remedy is not to convince people their speech is wrong but to widen who counts as authoritative, so that the costs of a stigmatized dialect fall away as the arrangements that impose them change. The question was never which grammar is correct. It was always whose.

questions.stem: The central thesis of the passage is that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: some dialects are genuinely more logical than others

choices.label: B
choices.text: linguistic correctness reflects social power rather than logical superiority, though its social effects are real

choices.label: C
choices.text: the distinction between correct and incorrect grammar has no practical consequences

choices.label: D
choices.text: speakers of stigmatized dialects should simply adopt the prestige dialect

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author argues correctness is 'a social verdict wearing the costume of a linguistic one' yet insists 'it matters enormously, precisely because the verdict is social.' A is wrong because the author says 'no dialect is broken' and none is more logical. C is wrong because the author stresses the distinction 'matters enormously' in schools, interviews, and courtrooms. D is wrong because the author's remedy is to 'widen who counts as authoritative,' not to have speakers simply switch dialects.

passageSets.title: The Comforts of Comparative Advantage
passageSets.passage:
Two centuries ago, economists produced an argument for free trade so elegant that it still functions as the discipline's proudest export. The doctrine of comparative advantage holds that even a nation clumsy at producing everything can gain by specializing in whatever it produces least badly and trading for the rest. What matters is not whether a country is better than its partners in absolute terms but where its own relative strengths lie. The conclusion has the pleasing shape of a proof: trade, properly understood, is not a contest with winners and losers but a shared enlargement of the pie.

The logic is genuinely powerful, and the passage does not dispute its core. If England sacrifices less by making cloth than by making wine, and Portugal the reverse, then each can consume more of both by specializing and exchanging. The model exposes the crude error of thinking that a nation must 'beat' others at production to benefit from commerce, and it has armed generations of reformers against the seductive, ruinous appeal of the tariff.

But elegance can quietly smuggle in assumptions that the world does not honor. The proof speaks of nations as if they were single persons who consume the gains, when in truth a country contains weavers and vintners whose fortunes diverge. When cheaper cloth arrives from abroad, the nation's total wealth may rise even as the displaced weaver's wages fall to nothing. The theorem promises that the winners gain more than the losers lose, and so could in principle compensate them; it does not promise that they will. A gain that is real in the aggregate can be, for particular people in particular towns, an unambiguous loss.

Nor does the model dwell on time. Its comparisons are static, a snapshot of who is relatively good at what today. Yet a country that specializes according to present advantage may forgo the industries in which it might have grown skilled tomorrow, trading a dynamic future for a comfortable present. The infant industry, protected for a season, sometimes matures into a strength that pure specialization would have strangled in the cradle. What is optimal in the snapshot need not be optimal across the film.

To notice these limits is not to endorse protectionism, whose costs the author regards as mostly genuine and often severe. It is rather to insist that a theorem is only as trustworthy as its premises, and that the premises here concern distribution and time in ways the elegant statement tends to hide. The doctrine remains one of the great achievements of economic reasoning. But its very beauty can tempt its admirers to mistake a claim about aggregate efficiency for a claim about justice, and to answer the anxious weaver with a proof he has every reason to distrust.

The wider lesson reaches beyond trade to the seductions of elegant reasoning as such. A proof that flatters us with its symmetry can quietly narrow the questions we think to ask, so that we notice the enlarged pie and overlook the hands from which particular slices were taken. To honor the theorem is not to recite it but to test its premises against the world, remembering that a conclusion valid within a model becomes a guide to policy only when the model's assumptions about distribution and time actually hold in the case before us.

choices.label: C

C is wrong because the author stresses that particular people can suffer 'an unambiguous loss.' D is wrong because England and Portugal are merely an illustration, not the doctrine's scope.

passageSets.title: The Grammar of Dress
passageSets.passage:
To many sensible people, fashion is the very emblem of frivolity: a churn of arbitrary changes that costs money, wastes effort, and signifies nothing beyond the vanity of those caught up in it. The practical mind asks only that clothing keep out the cold and last the season, and regards the rest as a tax on the insecure. There is something bracing in this contempt, but it rests on a misunderstanding of what clothing does. Dress is not merely covering; it is a system of signs, and like any system of signs it carries meaning whether or not we intend it to.

Consider how readily we read one another. A uniform, a frayed collar, a scarf knotted just so: from such details we infer, rightly or wrongly, a person's trade, allegiances, and sense of occasion, and we do so in the moment before a word is spoken. These inferences are possible only because the meanings of garments are shared, learned along with the other codes of a culture. What counts as formal or shabby, daring or dull, is not written into the cloth but agreed upon by a community, and it shifts as the community does. This is why the same coat can read as respectable in one decade and comic in the next, though not a thread of it has changed.

Once we see dress as a language, the supposed frivolity looks different. Languages change constantly, and we do not mock speech for having fashions in phrasing. The turnover of styles is not proof that the whole enterprise is empty; it is what any living system of meaning does. And the person who claims to stand outside it, who wears the same plain garments in stern indifference to trend, has not escaped the system but made a particular statement within it, one that others will read as they read any other, as principle, or thrift, or a studied disregard that is its own kind of display. There is no position of pure silence; to be dressed at all is to say something.

None of this should make us naive about the darker side of the practice. A shared code of dress is also a mechanism of exclusion and status, a way of marking who belongs and who cannot afford to. Industries profit by manufacturing dissatisfaction, persuading people that last year's signals now condemn them, and the costs of keeping up fall hardest on those who can least bear them. The language of dress, like any language, can be used to wound, to gatekeep, and to sell, and to romanticize it as pure self-expression is to ignore the pressures that shape what any of us can plausibly wear.

The honest conclusion holds both truths at once. Dress is meaningful, a genuine and unavoidable medium through which we present ourselves and read others, and it is also a field of coercion, competition, and waste. To dismiss it as trivial is to miss how much it communicates; to celebrate it as free expression is to miss how tightly its vocabulary is policed. What we wear is a sentence we cannot decline to speak, composed in a language we did not invent and do not fully control.

passageSets.title: The Central Bank's Studied Distance
passageSets.passage:
It is a curious feature of many democracies that one of the most consequential levers of policy is deliberately placed beyond the reach of elected officials. The power to set interest rates and steer the supply of money, which can decide whether prices are stable and whether workers find jobs, is entrusted in country after country to a central bank insulated from the government of the day. To a citizen who believes that important decisions should answer to the ballot, the arrangement can look like a puzzle at best and a scandal at worst.

The rationale begins with a temptation. An elected leader has reason to want a burst of easy money before an election, when the resulting boom is felt at once and the inflation it seeds arrives only later, conveniently after the votes are counted. But citizens are not fools; if they expect their leaders to yield to this temptation, they come to expect inflation, and their expectation becomes self-fulfilling, raising prices without buying any lasting prosperity. A central bank that credibly refuses to play this game, because it need not court voters, can break the cycle and anchor expectations, which is why independent banks have tended, across many countries, to preside over lower and steadier inflation.

Against this stands a democratic objection that should not be waved away. Monetary policy is often described as merely technical, a matter for experts tuning a machine, but that description flatters it. To raise rates in order to cool inflation is also to slow hiring and put people out of work; to choose how to balance the pain of rising prices against the pain of unemployment is to make a value-laden judgment about whose interests come first. Locking so distributive a choice inside an unelected institution removes it from the contest of votes precisely where that contest might matter most.

The way through the difficulty is to see that independence comes in degrees and kinds. A democracy need not choose between handing everything to technocrats and subjecting every decision to the electoral weather. It can, through its elected legislature, set the bank's mandate, deciding whether the goal shall be price stability alone or a balance of stable prices and full employment, while leaving to insulated experts the technical task of pursuing that goal week by week. On this view the bank's distance from politics is bounded and accountable: the ends are chosen democratically, the means are executed independently.

So understood, the case for independence is strong but conditional. It rests on the bank remaining within the mandate its citizens have given it, and a bank that strays beyond that mandate, or that treats its insulation as license, invites exactly the politicization it was built to prevent. The studied distance from politics is not a virtue in itself but a device, to be judged by whether it serves the public purposes that justify it. Independence earns its keep only so long as it remembers that it was granted, and can be revised, by the very democracy it holds at arm's length.

The arrangement, in the end, is a wager that a democracy can be wise enough to bind its own hands, choosing in a calm moment the rules it will not be tempted to break in a heated one. Like a constitution, an independent bank is self-government at one remove, the people ruling themselves by pre-commitment rather than by moment-to-moment vote. Its legitimacy therefore depends on remaining recognizably the people's instrument, chosen by them, answerable in its aims to them, and revisable by them, rather than a power that has forgotten whose it is.

passageSets.title: The Persistence of Ceremony
passageSets.passage:
A confident strand of modern thought predicted that ritual would fade as reason advanced. Ceremony, on this view, was a relic of superstition, a set of empty gestures that a clear-thinking people would eventually discard in favor of plain speech and practical action. The prediction has not come true. Secular societies that have abandoned the old religious calendars have promptly invented new occasions to stand in rows, recite fixed words, light candles, and fall silent together. Graduations, memorials, anniversaries, oaths of office, moments of silence for the dead: the forms multiply even among people who would deny believing in anything a ritual is supposed to invoke. This stubborn persistence is a fact in search of an explanation.

The rationalist critique assumes that a ritual is a kind of statement, and judges it by whether the statement is true or useful. Measured that way, ceremony fails: lighting a candle changes nothing about the world, and reciting a vow adds no information anyone lacked. But this is to misunderstand what a ritual is for. A ritual does not primarily assert; it does. Its repetitions and formalities are not clumsy attempts to communicate a proposition but a technology for accomplishing something speech alone cannot, namely the coordination of feeling and the public marking of a change in status. What matters is less what the words mean than what performing them together, in a prescribed way, brings about.

Consider how a ceremony converts a private fact into a shared one. Two people may already love each other, but the wedding makes their union a matter of public record and collective acknowledgment, binding not only them but everyone who witnesses it. A death is absorbed differently when mourners gather and enact a common form than when each grieves alone. The very features the critic mocks, the fixed script, the repetition, the resistance to improvisation, are what allow a crowd to feel and act as one rather than as a scatter of separate individuals. A ritual works precisely because it is not spontaneous; its familiarity is what lets people join it without hesitation.

None of this should tip into a blanket defense of every ceremony. Rituals can ossify into hollow routine, can be used to enforce hierarchies or to paper over injustice with a show of unity, and a form that no longer answers to any real feeling deserves to be questioned or retired. The point is not that ritual is always benign but that it is not the irrational residue the rationalist supposes. Its logic is simply a different logic, one of enactment rather than assertion, and dismissing it as empty misreads the kind of thing it is.

The persistence of ceremony in a disenchanted age therefore tells us something about human beings that the rationalist forecast missed. We are not only reasoning creatures who exchange propositions; we are social animals who need to mark time, to grieve and celebrate in company, and to feel our membership in something larger made tangible. Argument cannot supply that, and a society that tried to live by argument alone would find itself quietly reinventing the very forms it had declared obsolete. Ritual endures not in spite of our modernity but because modernity left a need it never learned to meet.

passageSets.title: An Income for Everyone
passageSets.passage:
The proposal sounds at first like a utopian dream or a fiscal absurdity: pay every citizen, rich and poor alike, a regular sum sufficient to cover basic needs, with no conditions attached and no questions asked. A universal basic income would replace the tangle of means-tested programs, each with its forms and thresholds and armies of administrators, with a single unconditional payment. Its champions span the political spectrum, an unusual coalition that should make us curious about what such different people think they see in it.

Part of the appeal is a critique of the welfare systems we already have. Programs that phase out as income rises can trap their recipients, since earning a little more may mean losing benefits worth more than the raise, so that the poor face effective tax rates the wealthy would never tolerate. A payment that never phases out removes this perverse penalty on work. And because it is universal, it carries none of the stigma that attaches to aid marked for the poor, nor the intrusive policing of how recipients live, which conditional programs so often require.

Skeptics raise two objections that any honest advocate must face. The first is cost: a payment large enough to matter, multiplied by an entire population, reaches sums that strain even wealthy treasuries, and the taxes needed to fund it might undo some of the good it does. The second is the worry that a guaranteed income, detached from any requirement to work, would erode the willingness to work at all, leaving fewer hands to produce the wealth the payments distribute. Both objections are serious, and neither can be waved away with slogans.

The evidence, though limited, complicates the darker predictions. In the trials conducted so far, recipients of unconditional cash have not, by and large, stopped working; the reductions in labor that appear are modest and concentrated among the young, who stay in school longer, and new parents, who spend more time with infants, choices few would call idleness. What the money more often buys is a measure of security, the ability to refuse the worst jobs, to train for better ones, or to weather a crisis without ruin. The specter of mass indolence has not, so far, materialized.

None of this proves the case, for pilots are not permanent policies, and effects that stay small at modest scale might grow at large scale. The honest verdict is that the idea is neither the panacea its enthusiasts promise nor the folly its critics fear, but a serious proposal whose merits turn on details its slogans obscure: how large the payment, financed by which taxes, replacing which existing programs. Those who debate the principle in the abstract, for or against, are usually arguing about a policy that no one has actually specified.

What the disagreement finally reveals is how much the abstract argument depends on numbers no slogan supplies. The same idea can name a lean grant that merely replaces today's tangle of programs or a lavish one that would remake the fiscal state, and these are not variations on a policy but different policies wearing one label. To debate them as if they were interchangeable is to argue past one another, and the honest work lies not in blessing or damning the principle but in specifying, and then testing, the concrete design the principle would take.

passageSets.title: Nothing to Hide
passageSets.passage:
A common argument holds that privacy matters only to those with something to conceal. If you have done nothing wrong, the reasoning goes, you should not mind being watched, and the honest citizen has no reason to fear surveillance that catches only wrongdoers. The claim has an intuitive pull, and it has been used to justify a great expansion of monitoring in workplaces, streets, and the digital spaces where much of modern life now happens. Yet the argument rests on a cramped picture of what privacy is for.

The picture treats privacy as a synonym for secrecy, a shield behind which people hide misdeeds. But much of what we keep private is not shameful at all. We close the door not because what happens inside is wrong but because being observed changes it: intimacy, candor, and play wither under a watching eye, and a self that is never unobserved never fully relaxes into being itself. Privacy is less the concealment of bad acts than the room in which ordinary, blameless life can be lived without the distorting pressure of an audience.

There is also a social dimension the argument misses. A society in which everyone is watched is not merely one in which criminals are caught; it is one in which dissent is chilled, in which people hesitate to explore unpopular ideas, join unusual causes, or say what authorities might dislike, because they cannot be sure how a record of their behavior will someday be read. The value of privacy here is not personal comfort but the breathing space a free society requires, and its loss is felt even by those who never intended to break any rule.

Moreover, the innocent have more to fear from total surveillance than the slogan admits. Information gathered for one purpose can be turned to another; a fact that is harmless today can be damaging under a different regime or a different interpretation, and the person with 'nothing to hide' has no guarantee that the standard by which he is judged will remain the one he assumed. To surrender privacy on the promise that only the guilty suffer is to trust that power will always be used as its holders currently say it will, a trust history rarely rewards.

None of this means that surveillance is never justified or that privacy must be absolute; there are genuine goods, safety among them, that can warrant limits. The point is narrower: that the 'nothing to hide' argument misunderstands the value at stake, treating a broad social and personal good as if it were a mere refuge for the guilty. Once privacy is properly understood, the burden shifts to those who would erode it, who must show that what is gained is worth what the slogan so casually gives away.

Properly understood, privacy is not a refuge for wrongdoing but a condition of ordinary flourishing, the room in which candor, intimacy, and unpopular thought can survive the distorting pressure of an audience. That is why the slogan fails even for the blameless: a watched society chills the dissent and experiment on which its own freedom depends, and information gathered innocently today can be turned against a person under a standard he never agreed to. Once the value at stake is seen clearly, the burden shifts to those who would erode it, to show that what is gained is worth what the phrase so casually gives away.

passageSets.title: The Gale of Creative Destruction
passageSets.passage:
Capitalism, one influential economist argued, is not a placid state of equilibrium but a perpetual storm. Its essential fact is not competition over price among firms selling the same thing, but the recurring arrival of the new: the new product, the new method, the new form of organization that does not merely undercut the old but renders it obsolete. This process, which he called creative destruction, is on his account the engine of rising living standards and, at the same time, the source of much of the disruption that makes capitalism so restless and so resented.

The insight reframes what looks like waste. When a new technology sweeps away an entire industry, the shuttered factories and idled workers appear to be pure loss, and in the short run, for those workers, they are. But the same wave that destroys also creates, on this view, releasing labor and capital from uses that have become inferior and channeling them, in time, toward more productive ones. The gaslighter displaced by electricity, the scribe displaced by the press, are casualties of a process that left their societies richer, and to freeze the economy in order to protect existing jobs would be to trade tomorrow's abundance for today's familiarity.

This is a bracing argument, and it contains a durable truth: economies that suppress the new in the name of stability tend to stagnate, and much of what we enjoy exists because some older way of doing things was allowed to die. Yet the theory can be deployed too smoothly, as though the word 'destruction' dissolved all obligation to those it destroys. That the process enriches the whole in the long run is cold comfort to the worker whose skill, painstakingly acquired, is rendered worthless at fifty, in a place where the new jobs do not appear and the old ones will not return.

The distribution of the gains and losses is the crux the cheerful version omits. The benefits of creative destruction are broad, arriving as cheaper and better goods for millions who never see the mechanism; the costs are concentrated, falling on particular workers in particular towns whose lives are upended so that the rest may prosper. To acknowledge the process as an engine of progress is not to accept that its victims should bear its costs alone, and a society can embrace dynamism while still deciding to share the burdens it imposes rather than leaving them where they happen to fall.

The mature attitude thus holds two thoughts at once that the slogans keep apart. Creative destruction is genuinely creative, and a society that shackles it will pay in lost prosperity; and it is genuinely destructive, and a society that ignores its casualties will pay in justice and, eventually, in the political backlash that neglected losers reliably produce. To celebrate the creation while denying the destruction is not realism but evasion.

The hardest discipline the idea demands is to hold its two halves together when every incentive tempts us to drop one. The winners from innovation, being many and content, rarely organize to praise it; the losers, being concentrated and aggrieved, reliably organize to resist it, and a politics that heard only the second would freeze the economy, while one that heard only the first would abandon its casualties. To keep both in view is not weakness but realism, and it is the only footing from which a society can enjoy the creation without being poisoned by the destruction it entails.

passageSets.title: The World Turned Upside Down
passageSets.passage:
At certain seasons many societies suspend their own rules. The servant plays the master, the fool is crowned king for a day, solemn hierarchies are mocked, and behavior forbidden the rest of the year is not merely tolerated but required. Such festivals of misrule can look, to the outsider, like a society briefly losing its mind, an outbreak of disorder that authority tolerates only because it cannot prevent it. Yet their very regularity suggests something more deliberate than collapse: the disorder is scheduled, bounded, and, in its way, as rule-governed as the order it inverts.

One influential reading treats the festival as a safety valve. A community that demands discipline all year accumulates resentments, and the carnival lets them escape harmlessly, venting the pressures that might otherwise burst the vessel. On this view the temporary inversion is profoundly conservative: by allowing the low to mock the high for a day, it reconciles them to obeying the high for the other three hundred and sixty-four. The release is permitted precisely because it secures the return to order.

A rival reading is less comfortable for the authorities. The festival, it suggests, does not only discharge discontent but rehearses alternatives, showing in play that the established order is not the only conceivable one. When the world is turned upside down for a day, everyone sees that it can be turned, that the arrangement of high and low is a human construction and not a law of nature, and that vision can outlast the holiday. What is meant as a release can become, on occasion, a rehearsal for revolt.

The two readings need not exclude each other, and their coexistence is what makes the festival so interesting. The same event can, in different circumstances, cement the order it mocks or corrode it, and which effect prevails may depend on conditions the ritual itself does not determine. A carnival in a stable and confident society may indeed be a harmless valve; the same carnival in a society already straining may light a fuse. The ambiguity is not a flaw in the analysis but a feature of the thing analyzed.

To understand such rituals, then, is to resist the tidy conclusion that they are simply one thing. Ordered disorder is a genuine paradox, a permitted transgression that both affirms and questions the rules it suspends, and its meaning is not fixed once and for all but negotiated anew each time the season comes around. The society that turns its world upside down is doing something more complex than either releasing steam or plotting rebellion; it is holding both possibilities in suspension, and the outcome is never guaranteed in advance.

The festival's meaning cannot be fixed once and for all because it genuinely holds two possibilities in suspension. The same day of scheduled misrule can vent resentments and reconcile the low to their obedience, or it can reveal that the order of high and low is a human arrangement that might be otherwise, and which effect prevails depends on conditions the ritual does not itself control. To insist that the carnival is simply one thing, pure safety valve or pure rehearsal for revolt, is to flatten a paradox that is not a flaw in the analysis but a feature of the thing.

passageSets.title: When Governments Fail
passageSets.passage:
Where markets stumble, the reflex of many is to summon the state. If pollution goes unpriced, if the poor go unhoused, if firms grow too powerful, government can step in to correct what private exchange gets wrong. This impulse rests on a comparison, usually unspoken, between a flawed market and an idealized government imagined as a benevolent, all-seeing corrector, moved only by the public interest and equipped to act on it. The comparison is not fair, and recognizing why is the beginning of wisdom about both.

For governments are not staffed by selfless philosopher-kings but by ordinary people, politicians seeking reelection, officials guarding their budgets, voters pursuing their own interests, and these people respond to incentives just as those in markets do. A tradition of analysis insists that we study government with the same unsentimental eye we turn on business, asking not what an ideal state would do but what actual states, peopled by self-interested agents, are likely to do. Seen this way, government has its own characteristic failures, as real as the market failures it is called upon to cure.

The list is sobering. Concentrated interests can capture policy at the expense of the diffuse public; programs, once created, acquire constituencies that keep them alive long after their purpose has passed; politicians favor visible benefits now over invisible costs later; and the knowledge required to plan well is often scattered beyond any planner's reach. None of these is an accident to be fixed by electing better people, for they flow from the structure of the situation, from the incentives that face anyone who holds the office, whatever his character.

Yet this analysis, valuable as a corrective, can itself be pushed too far. To show that government fails in characteristic ways is not to show that it always fails, or that markets never do, and the same skeptical eye that exposes the myth of the benevolent state should be turned on the rival myth of the flawless market. The honest question is never whether government is perfect, which it is not, but whether, in a particular case, imperfect government action would improve on an imperfect market outcome, a comparison between two flawed things rather than between one flawed thing and an ideal.

What the analysis finally teaches is a symmetry. Both markets and governments are made of self-interested people responding to incentives, and both can fail; the choice between them in any case is a choice between imperfect instruments, to be made by comparing their actual, not idealized, performance. The mistake to avoid is the double standard that judges the market by its real failures and the state by its imagined perfections, or the reverse. Clear thinking requires holding both to the same demanding standard.

The whole point is a symmetry that partisans of either side find uncomfortable. Markets are made of self-interested people responding to incentives, and so are governments, and both can fail in characteristic, structural ways that no change of personnel will cure. The mature question is never whether some idealized institution would perform perfectly but whether this flawed instrument would, in this case, improve on that flawed one. To judge either by its real record and the other by its imagined perfection is the double standard that lets clear thinking collapse into cheerleading.

passageSets.title: The Small Change of Civility
passageSets.passage:
Manners are easy to despise. They can seem like empty forms, the fussy residue of vanished hierarchies, a set of arbitrary rules whose main function is to separate those who know them from those who do not. The reformer impatient with hypocrisy is tempted to sweep them away in favor of plain sincerity, on the theory that a good heart needs no etiquette and that politeness is often a mask for its opposite. There is enough truth in this suspicion to give it force, for manners have indeed been used to exclude, and courtesy can indeed cloak contempt.

And yet a society that actually abolished its manners would discover how much work they had quietly been doing. Manners are the small change of social life, the low-denomination currency in which countless daily transactions are conducted without anyone having to negotiate terms afresh each time. The conventions of greeting, of turn-taking in talk, of the thousand tiny signals by which we show that we mean no harm, allow strangers to deal with one another smoothly precisely because the forms are settled and need not be invented on the spot. Where such forms are absent, every trivial encounter threatens to become a fraught negotiation.

The deeper function of manners is to extend to others a presumption of regard that we cannot possibly feel, in each case, sincerely. We are told to be polite to people we do not know and may not like, and the demand is sometimes derided as insincerity. But this misunderstands the point. Manners are precisely what allow us to treat others decently in the absence of feeling, supplying a floor of consideration that does not depend on the accident of whether we happen to be fond of the person before us. Far from being the enemy of genuine regard, they are its everyday substitute and, often, its training ground.

This is why the charge of hypocrisy, though not baseless, cuts less deep than it seems. To hold a door for someone one privately dislikes is not a lie; it is an acknowledgment that the other's claim to basic courtesy does not hinge on one's private feelings. A world in which people were polite only when they sincerely felt warm would be a world in which the unloved, the irritating, and the merely unfamiliar received no consideration at all. The 'insincerity' of manners is what universalizes a decency that sincerity alone would ration to our friends.

None of this requires defending every particular convention, many of which are indeed arbitrary or outdated and can be discarded without loss. The point concerns manners as such, the very institution of settled forms of mutual regard, rather than any specific rule of which fork to use. To dismiss the whole for the failings of its parts is to mistake the dispensable content of etiquette for its indispensable function, and to risk tearing down, in the name of sincerity, one of the quiet structures that lets people who owe each other nothing still treat each other as though they did.

Manners deserve defense not in their every particular, many of which are arbitrary and may be discarded, but as an institution, the settled forms by which people who owe each other nothing still treat each other as though they did. They extend a presumption of regard we cannot sincerely feel toward everyone, universalizing a decency that sincerity alone would ration to our friends. To tear down the whole for the failings of its parts, in the name of a purer honesty, is to mistake the dispensable content of etiquette for its indispensable function, and to lose a quiet structure that holds shared life together.

passageSets.title: The Hazard of Being Insured
passageSets.passage:
Insurance is one of the great inventions for taming risk, pooling the misfortunes of many so that no single person is ruined by a stroke of bad luck. Yet the very protection it provides creates a subtle problem that has fascinated students of economics: the fact that being insured against a risk can change how carefully one guards against it. A person who knows his losses will be covered may take less care to avoid them, and this altered behavior, called moral hazard, is woven into the logic of insurance itself.

The mechanism is not usually deliberate wickedness but a quiet shift in incentives. The careful precautions that once protected one's own uninsured wealth become, after insurance, precautions taken on behalf of the insurer, and human beings reliably take less trouble to protect what is no longer fully theirs to lose. The driver whose car is fully covered may park a little less carefully; the business whose losses are insured may invest a little less in prevention. None of these people need be dishonest; they are simply responding, as people do, to a changed structure of costs and benefits.

The problem is genuine, but it is often misdescribed as a reason to distrust insurance itself, which would be a serious error. The gains from pooling risk are enormous, and a world without insurance would be one in which every misfortune fell with full force on its victim, a far crueler and less productive arrangement. The existence of moral hazard is not an argument against insuring risks but a challenge to design insurance so that it protects against misfortune without wholly dissolving the incentive to avoid it.

And design can do much. Requiring the insured to bear part of each loss, through deductibles and co-payments, restores some of the stake that pure coverage removes, so that the insured still has reason to be careful even while protected against catastrophe. Charging lower premiums to those who take precautions, or monitoring behavior where feasible, aligns the incentives further. These devices do not abolish moral hazard, which is inherent, but they contain it, striking a balance between the security insurance offers and the vigilance it would otherwise erode.

The concept, moreover, reaches far beyond commercial insurance to any arrangement that shields people from the consequences of their choices. A guarantee that failing firms will be rescued may encourage the very recklessness that leads to failure; a promise to forgive every debt may loosen the caution that debt requires. In each case the lesson is the same: protection changes behavior, and any policy that softens the consequences of risk must reckon with the possibility that it will, at the same time, quietly encourage the risk. To ignore this is not compassion but carelessness, and to overstate it is not prudence but a willingness to leave the unlucky unprotected.

The concept reaches far beyond commercial insurance to any arrangement that shields people from the consequences of their choices, from bailouts that may breed the recklessness they rescue to guarantees that loosen the caution risk requires. In each case the lesson holds: protection changes behavior, and a policy that softens the consequences of a risk must reckon with the chance that it will, at the same time, quietly encourage it. To ignore this is not compassion but carelessness, and to overstate it is not prudence but a willingness to leave the genuinely unlucky without the shelter they deserve.

passageSets.title: The Stranger in Our Midst
passageSets.passage:
There is a peculiar social figure who is neither an insider nor quite an outsider: the stranger who comes today and stays tomorrow. Unlike the traveler who passes through, this figure settles among a group without fully belonging to it, near enough to participate yet distant enough to remain, in some sense, apart. The trader who takes up residence in a town, the migrant who joins a community, the newcomer who never sheds the mark of having come from elsewhere, all occupy this ambiguous position, and the position itself, quite apart from any individual who fills it, has distinctive social effects.

The stranger's defining feature is a particular blend of nearness and remoteness. He is near, because he lives among the group and shares its daily life; he is remote, because he did not grow from its soil and carries the memory of other places and other ways. This combination gives him a vantage the native lacks. He sees the group's habits as habits rather than as the natural order of things, noticing what its members take for granted precisely because he does not share their assumptions, and this outsider's clarity can make him a uniquely acute observer of the very community he has joined.

The same distance that clarifies can also be entrusted. Because the stranger stands somewhat apart from the group's rivalries and factions, he is often trusted with confidences the members would not share with one another, and asked to judge disputes in which a native could not appear impartial. The figure who does not fully belong can, for that reason, be given roles that require standing outside the ordinary web of loyalties, becoming the confidant, the mediator, the one to whom things are said precisely because he is not quite one of us.

Yet the stranger's position carries an inherent precariousness. His usefulness depends on his remaining somewhat apart, and that same apartness makes him a ready target when the group turns fearful or seeks someone to blame. In good times his difference is an asset, a source of fresh perspective and trusted neutrality; in bad times the very same difference marks him out, and the qualities that made him valuable can, without his changing at all, make him suspect. The stranger is welcomed and resented for the same reason: that he is among the group without being wholly of it.

To study the stranger is thus to study a permanent feature of social life, not a mere accident of particular histories. Every society generates this position, and the roles it assigns to those who fill it, observer, confidant, scapegoat, reveal how the group manages the boundary between itself and what lies beyond. The stranger is a mirror in which a community can see the shape of its own edges, and how it treats him, in seasons of confidence and of fear alike, tells us as much about the group as about the newcomer it has taken in but not quite absorbed.

The stranger is thus a permanent feature of social life rather than an accident of particular histories, and the roles a group assigns to him, observer, confidant, and, in fearful seasons, scapegoat, reveal how it manages the boundary between itself and what lies beyond. His usefulness and his vulnerability spring from the same source, for the distance that lets him see clearly and judge impartially also marks him out when the group turns anxious. How a community treats the one who is among it without being wholly of it tells us, in the end, as much about the community as about the newcomer.

passageSets.title: The Pursuit of Unearned Gain
passageSets.passage:
There are two broad ways to grow rich. One is to produce something others value, a better product, a cheaper service, a useful innovation, and to profit by offering it in exchange. The other is to capture a larger share of wealth that already exists, not by creating anything but by manipulating the rules, securing a privilege, or extracting a payment for granting or withholding permission. Economists call the second activity rent-seeking, and distinguishing it from genuine production is essential to understanding why some economies flourish and others stagnate.

The distinction matters because the two activities, though both aimed at profit, have opposite effects on the wealth of a society. Productive effort enlarges the pie: the innovator who builds a better tool leaves the world with a tool it did not have before, and his gain is drawn from value he helped create. Rent-seeking merely redistributes the pie, and worse, consumes resources in the redistributing. The lobbyist who wins his client a protected monopoly, the official who must be paid to issue a license, the incumbent who erects legal barriers against competitors, all grow richer without adding anything, and the effort they pour into the contest is, from society's view, pure waste.

What makes rent-seeking especially corrosive is that it is often perfectly rational for the individual even as it is ruinous for the whole. A firm that can earn more by lobbying for favors than by improving its products will, quite sensibly, hire lobbyists rather than engineers, and once some firms do so, others must follow or be disadvantaged. Talent and capital that might have gone into creating value are drawn instead into the struggle over shares, and a society can find its best energies devoted not to building but to jockeying for position at the trough.

The problem is compounded by its invisibility, for rent-seeking usually dresses itself in the language of the public good. The barrier against competitors is defended as protecting consumers or preserving quality; the special privilege is justified as serving some worthy end; and the true purpose, to secure an unearned advantage, is rarely announced. Disentangling genuine public-interest regulation from disguised rent-seeking is genuinely hard, which is exactly what makes the latter so effective: it thrives in the difficulty of telling it apart from the former.

The health of an economy, on this view, depends heavily on which of the two paths to wealth it makes more rewarding. Where the surest route to riches is to produce, talent flows into production and the society grows prosperous; where the surest route is to capture, talent flows into capture and the society stagnates, however clever its rent-seekers may be. A central task of good institutions is thus to make production pay better than manipulation, channeling self-interest toward creating wealth rather than toward the elaborate and wasteful contest over dividing what already exists.

The health of an economy depends heavily on which path to wealth it makes the more rewarding. Where the surest route to riches is to produce something others value, talent flows into production and the society grows prosperous; where the surest route is to capture a larger share by manipulating the rules, talent flows into capture and the society stagnates, however clever its rent-seekers may be. A central task of good institutions is thus to make production pay better than manipulation, channeling self-interest toward creating wealth rather than toward the wasteful contest over dividing what already exists.
